summary: I was born in Lincoln, Nebraska, on January 7, 1909. My parents moved there from Butte, Montana, because my father wanted to live in a place with more African-Americans. Lincoln was where I grew up, and my father bought a house and gardened there while working as a train waiter.

My parents were living in Butte, Montana, around the time I was conceived, but my father was uneasy about staying there since there were no African-Americans in that area. So they decided to move to Lincoln, Nebraska, before I was born. My father’s work as a train waiter had him traveling through the Northwest, which is how he knew the region well.

I grew up during a time when many immigrants were coming to the United States, especially from Europe. My mother spoke German and hired a German-speaking helper to assist her at home. This was part of the immigrant influx that shaped the communities we lived in, like Lincoln, Nebraska, and places in Kansas.

My mother knew how to speak German, which was quite useful because during the period when many immigrants came to the United States, she hired a German-speaking helper to assist with household tasks. This helped bridge language gaps in our community. I believe my mother’s education and upbringing in Kansas contributed to her language skills.

My mother was born in Fort Scott, Kansas, the same place as Gordon Parks. Although she was born there, she was raised mainly in Leavenworth, Kansas. Fort Scott is part of my family’s roots and connects to the broader story of African-American families in Kansas.

Gordon Parks was born in the same place as my mother, Fort Scott, Kansas. While I didn’t have a personal relationship with him, it’s interesting that we share that connection through our families’ origins. It highlights the significance of Fort Scott in the African-American community of that era.

My mother was raised in Leavenworth, Kansas, and she sang grand opera there locally. My father’s family also had property in Leavenworth, so it was an important place for both sides of my family. Leavenworth shaped much of my mother’s upbringing and cultural life.
